did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to classify melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions. The study trains CNN models on raw skin lesion images and sonified images (waveforms and spectrograms) to evaluate their performance in diagnosing melanoma, a dermatology task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) on raw, sonified, and spectrogram images for melanoma diagnosis. It includes a methodology section detailing the CNN architecture and sonification process, a results section with specific performance metrics (accuracy, loss, F1-score) from training experiments, and a survey of 50 participants regarding data sharing willingness. The publication venue is BIC 2021, a conference, and the content follows the structure of an original research study rather than a literature review.

title: Privacy-preserving With Sonification For Training of Convolutional Deep Neural Networks for Melanoma Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study investigates using sonification to convert raw skin lesion images into waveforms and spectrograms to address privacy concerns in melanoma diagnosis. The researchers trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) on 14,243 images of melanoma and non-melanoma lesions to compare performance against models trained on raw images. Evaluation metrics included validation accuracy, loss, and F1-score, with the best waveform model achieving 79% validation accuracy compared to 83% for raw images. Additionally, a survey of 50 participants revealed that sonification increased willingness to share data for public AI training by 16% compared to raw images. The results indicate that while sonified images yield slightly lower accuracy, they offer a viable privacy-preserving alternative that encourages greater data sharing for robust AI development.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis. The dataset contained 7,122 images of Melanoma. The study focuses on automated diagnosis of melanoma to improve survival rates through early detection.
The comparator class used in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 7,121 images of Non-Melanoma. The paper distinguishes between malignant melanoma and benign/non-melanoma lesions.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task to diagnose melanoma. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (Malignant Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma/Benign Lesions) using Convolutional Neural Networks trained on raw images, waveforms, and spectrograms. Performance was evaluated using validation accuracy, loss, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: Melanoma and Non-Melanoma Skin Lesion Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing raw skin lesion images used for training and validation of Convolutional Neural Networks for melanoma diagnosis. The study notes that the data was limited to patients of Caucasian descent.

use.training: Used to train CNN models on raw images, sonified waveforms, and sonified spectrograms.
use.validation: Used to evaluate model performance (accuracy, loss, F1-score) during training.
use.other: The sonified versions (waveforms and spectrograms) generated from this dataset were also used for training and validation. The study mentions generating more than 10,000 waveform and spectrogram images from this source.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models trained on raw, sonified, and spectrogram images were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, loss, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

experiment_20k_steps.description: Model performance metrics after 20,000 training steps with validation batch size of 40.
experiment_20k_steps.notes: At 20k steps, normal images showed 78-79% validation accuracy. Waveforms showed 76% validation accuracy. Spectrograms showed 56-66% validation accuracy depending on batch size.

experiment_50k_steps.description: Model performance metrics after 50,000 training steps with validation batch size of 40.
experiment_50k_steps.notes: At 50k steps, normal images achieved 82-83% validation accuracy. Waveforms achieved 78-79% validation accuracy (a ~4% gap). Spectrograms achieved 70-75% validation accuracy (an ~8% gap compared to normal images). The waveform model with batch size 10 showed 79% validation accuracy, 52% loss, and 77% F1 score.

survey_results.description: Survey of 50 participants regarding willingness to share skin images for AI training.

metrics.public_database_sharing_increase: 16% more participants willing to share sonified images compared to original images to a public database.
metrics.private_database_sharing_increase: 12% more participants willing to share sonified images compared to original images to a restricted private database.
metrics.total_sharing_increase: 4% more participants willing to share sonified images for AI training in total (public or private).

survey_results.notes: Sonification encourages public disclosure. Participants were much more confident sharing sonified images to the public database.

architecture_used.pretraining: None reported for the primary study models; a separate study [7] using Inception V2 pretrained on ImageNet is mentioned for comparison.

layers.description: Two activated fully connected layers followed by a third layer with softmax equation resulting in 2 output values

training_details.normalization: RGB channels normalized to a mean value of 0.5
training_details.data_loading: Batches of 8 samples during training, 50 samples during validation

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep Ensemble for Recognition of Melanoma (DERM)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a uniform size of 224x224 pixels
Normalization of RGB channels to a mean value of 0.5
Sonification: Transformation of images into audio (wav files) and then into waveform and spectrogram images using Fourier Fast Transform
conclusions: Sonification of skin lesion images significantly increases patient willingness to share data for public AI training, with 16% more participants willing to share sonified images to a public database compared to raw images.

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) trained on sonified waveform images achieve performance similar to those trained on raw images, with a minimal validation accuracy reduction of approximately 4% (79% for waveforms vs. 83% for raw images at 50,000 steps).

CNNs trained on sonified spectrogram images show a larger performance gap compared to raw images, with a validation accuracy reduction of approximately 8% (75% for spectrograms vs. 83% for raw images at 50,000 steps).
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the dataset's lack of diversity, noting that the data was limited to patients of Caucasian descent, which may affect generalization to other skin tones.
The study notes that image quality, particularly from mobile devices, likely affects the quality of sonification and subsequent model performance.
Sonification serves as a viable privacy-preserving alternative that encourages data sharing, potentially addressing the bottleneck of limited training data availability in melanoma diagnosis.
important_remarks: The dataset is limited to patients of Caucasian descent, which introduces population bias and limits generalizability to other skin tones.
The study lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing (p-values or confidence intervals) for performance comparisons between models.

Sonified spectrogram models showed significantly lower performance (70-75% validation accuracy) compared to raw images (82-83%), indicating a substantial performance gap for this specific sonification method.
The sonification process is computationally expensive, taking approximately one week to transform 14,243 images on a single-core machine, which may hinder scalability.
The survey on data sharing willingness was small (n=50) and self-reported, potentially limiting the robustness of the privacy benefit claims.
Image quality, particularly from mobile devices, was noted as a factor that likely affects the quality of sonification and subsequent model performance.

The study compares a custom CNN architecture against raw images but does not compare against state-of-the-art dermatology-specific models (e.g., DERM) in the primary sonification experiment, relying on literature values for comparison.
